---
title: "Docker Container Deep Dive: Every Layer, Every Choice"
author: "Evan McIntosh"
date: "2026-04-03"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
---

# Why Docker at All?

The `ws_contraction_px4` workspace contains ROS 2 nodes (Python and C++) that talk to
PX4 over DDS.  Building these nodes requires an exact stack:

- **ROS 2 Jazzy** (specific Ubuntu 24.04 release)
- **px4_msgs** compiled against the same RMW
- **acados** built from source with qpOASES
- **JAX**, immrax, linrax, casadi, equinox --- all at compatible versions

Installing all of this natively on your host is fragile and hard to reproduce.
Docker packages the entire build-and-run environment into a single image so that
`make build && make run` gives you a working controller workspace on any Linux
machine, every time.

Critically, the Docker container **does not** run PX4 or Gazebo.  Those stay on
the host.  The container only houses the ROS 2 controller nodes.  This split
keeps the image small and avoids GPU passthrough complexity.


# The Base Image

```dockerfile
FROM osrf/ros:jazzy-ros-base
```

This pulls the official Open Source Robotics Foundation image for ROS 2 Jazzy
built on Ubuntu 24.04.  The `ros-base` variant (~700 MB) includes only:

- `rclpy` / `rclcpp` (the Python and C++ ROS 2 client libraries)
- Core ROS 2 middleware (rmw, rcl, rosidl)
- `ament` / `colcon` build infrastructure

It **excludes** rviz2, rqt, perception stacks, Gazebo plugins, and every other
GUI or sensor package that `desktop-full` (~2.5 GB) would include.  Since the
container never renders anything, those packages would be dead weight.

### Shell configuration

```dockerfile
SHELL ["/bin/bash", "-c"]
```

Overrides the default `/bin/sh` so that every `RUN` instruction uses Bash.  This
is necessary because `source` (used to load ROS 2 setup files) is a Bash
built-in that does not exist in POSIX `sh`.

### Non-interactive frontend

```dockerfile
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
```

Tells `apt-get` to never prompt for user input (timezone, keyboard layout,
etc.).  Without this, certain packages would block the build waiting for a TTY
that does not exist.


# Layer 1 --- System Dependencies

```dockerfile
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    python3-pip python3-dev python3-colcon-common-extensions \
    git cmake build-essential \
    libgmp-dev libcdd-dev libeigen3-dev liblapack-dev libopenblas-dev \
    wget \
    && apt-get remove -y python3-matplotlib 2>/dev/null || true \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

Breaking this down package by package:

| Package | Why it is needed |
|---------|------------------|
| `python3-pip` | Installs Python packages not available as `.deb` (JAX, casadi, etc.) |
| `python3-dev` | C headers for building Python extensions (required by immrax, acados_template) |
| `python3-colcon-common-extensions` | `colcon build` --- the ROS 2 workspace build tool (not included in `ros-base`) |
| `git` | Cloning px4_msgs, acados, immrax, linrax during the build |
| `cmake`, `build-essential` | C/C++ compiler toolchain for acados and C++ ROS 2 packages |
| `libgmp-dev` | GNU Multiple Precision library --- dependency of `libcdd` |
| `libcdd-dev` | Convex hull / polytope library --- used by immrax for reachability |
| `libeigen3-dev` | Eigen 3 linear algebra headers --- used by C++ controllers and acados |
| `liblapack-dev`, `libopenblas-dev` | BLAS/LAPACK backends for scipy, acados QP solves |
| `wget` | Downloads the acados tera_renderer binary |

**matplotlib removal**: The base image ships `python3-matplotlib` as a system
package.  It is explicitly removed because (a) the container is headless --- no
display backend exists, and (b) pip later installs a newer matplotlib from
PyPI via `requirements.txt`.  The `|| true` ensures the build does not fail if
matplotlib was already absent.

**`--no-install-recommends`**: Prevents apt from pulling in hundreds of MB of
"recommended" packages (documentation, X11 libs, etc.) that are not needed.

**`rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`**: Deletes the apt package index cache after
installation.  This saves ~30 MB in the final image layer.  The cache would be
stale anyway since no further `apt-get install` runs after this point.


# Layer 2 --- px4_msgs (Pre-built ROS 2 Messages)

```dockerfile
RUN mkdir -p /opt/ws_px4_msgs/src && \
    git clone --depth 1 --branch v1.16_minimal_msgs \
        https://github.com/evannsmc/px4_msgs.git \
        /opt/ws_px4_msgs/src/px4_msgs && \
    source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash && \
    cd /opt/ws_px4_msgs && \
    colcon build --cmake-args -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release && \
    rm -rf /opt/ws_px4_msgs/build \
           /opt/ws_px4_msgs/log \
           /opt/ws_px4_msgs/src
```

### What this does

PX4 communicates with ROS 2 via **uORB messages** bridged through MicroXRCE-DDS.
On the ROS 2 side, these messages must be defined as ROS 2 `.msg` files and
compiled into Python/C++ bindings.  The `px4_msgs` package provides those
definitions.

### Why `v1.16_minimal_msgs`

This is your fork with only the messages actually used by the controllers
(`VehicleOdometry`, `VehicleStatus`, `RcChannels`, `OffboardControlMode`,
`TrajectorySetpoint`, `VehicleRatesSetpoint`, `VehicleCommand`).  The upstream
`px4_msgs` repo contains hundreds of message types; building all of them takes
minutes and wastes image space.

### Why pre-built inside the image

If px4_msgs were built inside `/workspace` (the bind-mounted host directory), it
would need to be rebuilt every time the container was recreated.  By building it
into `/opt/ws_px4_msgs/install/` during the Docker image build, it becomes a
permanent layer that never needs rebuilding.

### Why build artifacts are deleted

```dockerfile
rm -rf /opt/ws_px4_msgs/build /opt/ws_px4_msgs/log /opt/ws_px4_msgs/src
```

Only the `install/` directory is needed at runtime (it contains the compiled
`.so` libraries and Python packages).  The `build/` directory (CMake intermediates),
`log/` directory (build logs), and `src/` directory (source code) are no longer
needed and would waste image space.


# Layer 3 --- acados (Nonlinear MPC Solver)

```dockerfile
ENV ACADOS_ROOT=/opt/acados
RUN git clone https://github.com/acados/acados.git ${ACADOS_ROOT} && \
    cd ${ACADOS_ROOT} && \
    git checkout v0.5.1 && \
    git submodule update --recursive --init && \
    mkdir -p build && cd build && \
    cmake -DACADOS_WITH_QPOASES=ON \
          -CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=${ACADOS_ROOT} \
          .. && \
    make install -j$(nproc) && \
    rm -rf ${ACADOS_ROOT}/build
```

### What is acados?

acados is a high-performance solver for nonlinear optimal control problems.  The
NMPC controller in this workspace formulates a quadrotor trajectory-tracking
problem as an OCP (optimal control problem) and hands it to acados to solve at
each control step (~100 Hz).

### Build options explained

- **`v0.5.1`**: Pinned version for reproducibility.
- **`--recursive --init`**: acados vendors several submodules (BLASFEO, HPIPM,
  qpOASES).  These must be initialized before building.
- **`DACADOS_WITH_QPOASES=ON`**: Enables the qpOASES QP solver backend.  This
  is a dense active-set QP solver suitable for the small problem sizes in
  quadrotor NMPC (typically 12 states, 4 inputs, 50 shooting intervals).
- **`-j$(nproc)`**: Parallel compilation using all available CPU cores.
- **`rm -rf build`**: Removes CMake intermediates (object files, CMake cache)
  to save image space.  The installed libraries and headers remain.

### Environment variables

```dockerfile
ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH="${ACADOS_ROOT}/lib:${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}"
ENV ACADOS_SOURCE_DIR="${ACADOS_ROOT}"
```

- **`LD_LIBRARY_PATH`**: Tells the dynamic linker where to find `libacados.so`
  at runtime.
- **`ACADOS_SOURCE_DIR`**: Used by `acados_template` (the Python interface) to
  locate the acados installation and its code-generation templates.


# Layer 4 --- acados Python Interface + Tera Renderer

```dockerfile
RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages --ignore-installed \
        -e ${ACADOS_ROOT}/interfaces/acados_template && \
    mkdir -p ${ACADOS_ROOT}/bin && \
    wget -q https://github.com/acados/tera_renderer/releases/download/v0.0.34/\
t_renderer-v0.0.34-linux \
         -O ${ACADOS_ROOT}/bin/t_renderer && \
    chmod +x ${ACADOS_ROOT}/bin/t_renderer
```

### `acados_template`

This is acados's Python code-generation frontend.  When you call
`ensure_solver.py`, it:

1. Defines the OCP (dynamics, cost, constraints) in Python
2. Renders C code from Jinja2-like templates via `t_renderer`
3. Compiles the generated C code into a shared library
4. Wraps it with a Python interface for calling from ROS 2

### `t_renderer` (Tera Renderer)

A pre-built binary that renders Tera templates (Rust-based templating engine)
into C source files.  acados ships template files (`.in.c`, `.in.h`) that
contain the OCP structure; `t_renderer` fills in the specific dimensions, cost
matrices, and constraint bounds for your problem.

### pip flags

- **`--no-cache-dir`**: Does not store downloaded wheels.  Saves image space.
- **`--break-system-packages`**: Required on Ubuntu 24.04 (PEP 668) to install
  into the system Python without a virtual environment.  Safe here because we
  control the entire container.
- **`--ignore-installed`**: Forces reinstallation even if a version exists.
  Prevents conflicts with system-installed versions.
- **`-e` (editable)**: Installs acados_template as a symlink to its source
  directory so that any acados updates are immediately visible.


# Layer 5 --- Python Dependencies

```dockerfile
COPY docker/requirements.txt /tmp/requirements.txt
RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages \
        --ignore-installed -r /tmp/requirements.txt
```

The `requirements.txt` contains:

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `numpy` | Fundamental array library --- used everywhere |
| `scipy` | Integration (`solve_ivp`), optimization, linear algebra |
| `pandas` | CSV log reading/writing and post-flight analysis |
| `control` | Python Control Systems Library --- used for LQR design in contraction controller |
| `matplotlib` | Plotting (only used in analysis scripts, not during flight) |
| `jax[cpu]` | JAX with CPU backend --- automatic differentiation for immrax/linrax |
| `equinox` | Neural-network-style modules on JAX --- used by immrax |
| `jaxtyping` | Type annotations for JAX arrays |
| `pyJoules` | Energy measurement --- optional profiling of controller computation cost |
| `casadi` | Symbolic math + automatic differentiation --- used in NMPC formulation |

### Why `jax[cpu]`?

The `[cpu]` extra installs JAX without CUDA/GPU dependencies.  The immrax and
linrax reachability libraries use JAX for interval arithmetic and automatic
differentiation, but the computations are small enough that CPU is sufficient.
This avoids pulling in ~2 GB of CUDA libraries.


# Layer 6 --- immrax and linrax

```dockerfile
RUN git clone --depth 1 --branch jax-0.9-support \
        https://github.com/Akash-Harapanahalli/immrax.git /tmp/immrax && \
    sed -i 's|git@github.com:|https://github.com/|g' \
        /tmp/immrax/.gitmodules 2>/dev/null || true && \
    git -C /tmp/immrax submodule sync --recursive 2>/dev/null || true && \
    git -C /tmp/immrax submodule update --init --recursive 2>/dev/null || true && \
    pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages /tmp/immrax && \
    rm -rf /tmp/immrax
```

### What are these?

- **immrax**: Interval-arithmetic reachability analysis library built on JAX.
  Used by the contraction controller to compute reachable sets and contraction
  metrics.
- **linrax**: Linear relaxation library (also JAX-based).  Provides linear
  bounds on nonlinear dynamics for the contraction analysis.

### Why the `sed` on `.gitmodules`?

immrax's `.gitmodules` file uses SSH URLs (`git@github.com:...`).  Inside a
Docker build there are no SSH keys, so these would fail.  The `sed` command
rewrites them to HTTPS URLs which work without authentication for public repos.

### Why `jax-0.9-support` branch?

The main branches of these libraries target older JAX versions.  This branch
has compatibility patches for JAX 0.9.x which is what `requirements.txt`
installs.

### Cleanup

```dockerfile
rm -rf /tmp/immrax
rm -rf /tmp/linrax
```

Source repos are deleted after pip-installing since only the installed Python
packages are needed at runtime.


# Layer 7 --- Shell Setup (Source Chain)

```dockerfile
RUN echo "source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash" > /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh && \
    echo "source /opt/ws_px4_msgs/install/setup.bash" \
        >> /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh && \
    echo 'if [ -f /workspace/install/setup.bash ]; then \
        source /workspace/install/setup.bash; fi' \
        >> /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh && \
    echo "export ACADOS_SOURCE_DIR=${ACADOS_ROOT}" \
        >> /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh && \
    echo "export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=${ACADOS_ROOT}/lib:\${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}" \
        >> /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh && \
    echo "source /etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh" >> /root/.bashrc
```

### The overlay chain

ROS 2 uses an **overlay workspace** model.  Each `setup.bash` extends the
environment from the previous one.  The chain here is:

```
/opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash              # (1) Base ROS 2 install
    └── /opt/ws_px4_msgs/install/setup.bash   # (2) px4_msgs overlay
        └── /workspace/install/setup.bash     # (3) Your controller packages
```

Each overlay adds its packages to `AMENT_PREFIX_PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`,
`LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, etc., so that `ros2 run` can discover all installed packages.

### Why `/etc/profile.d/`?

Files in `/etc/profile.d/` are sourced by **login shells** (`bash -l` or
`bash -lc "..."`).  The Makefile runs all `docker exec` commands with
`bash -lc`, which triggers this sourcing automatically.

### Why also `.bashrc`?

`.bashrc` is sourced by **interactive non-login shells** (what you get with
`docker exec -it ... bash`).  Adding the source line there ensures the
environment is correct when you `make attach` for debugging.

### The conditional for `/workspace/install/`

```bash
if [ -f /workspace/install/setup.bash ]; then
    source /workspace/install/setup.bash
fi
```

When the container first starts (before `make build_ros`), `/workspace/install/`
does not exist yet.  Without the `if` guard, every shell would print an error
about the missing file.


# Workdir and Entrypoint

```dockerfile
WORKDIR /workspace
CMD ["bash"]
```

- **`WORKDIR /workspace`**: Sets the default directory for all subsequent
  commands.  When you `make attach`, you land in `/workspace`.
- **`CMD ["bash"]`**: The default process.  Combined with `docker run -itd`,
  this starts a Bash shell that keeps the container alive in the background,
  waiting for `docker exec` commands.


# How the Container Runs

```makefile
run:
    docker rm -f $(CONTAINER_NAME) 2>/dev/null || true
    docker run -itd --rm \
        --net host \
        -e ROS_DOMAIN_ID=$(ROS_DOMAIN_ID) \
        -v $(WS_ROOT):/workspace \
        --name $(CONTAINER_NAME) \
        $(IMAGE_NAME)
```

### Flag-by-flag breakdown

| Flag | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `docker rm -f ... \|\| true` | Kill any existing container with the same name (idempotent restart) |
| `-i` | Keep STDIN open (needed for interactive `docker exec` later) |
| `-t` | Allocate a pseudo-TTY (enables colored output, signal handling) |
| `-d` | Detach --- run in background, return control to host shell |
| `--rm` | Auto-remove the container when it stops (no stale containers) |
| `--net host` | Share the host's network namespace --- see below |
| `-e ROS_DOMAIN_ID=31` | Isolate DDS discovery to domain 31 |
| `-v $(WS_ROOT):/workspace` | Bind-mount the repo root into the container |
| `--name px4_controllers` | Fixed name so Makefile targets can reference it |

### `--net host` explained

Normally, Docker creates an isolated virtual network for each container.  ROS 2
uses DDS (Data Distribution Service) for communication, which relies on
multicast UDP for node discovery.  Multicast does not cross Docker's default
bridge network without complex configuration.

`--net host` eliminates the problem entirely: the container shares the host's
network stack.  From a networking perspective, processes inside the container are
indistinguishable from host processes.  This means:

- The controller node's DDS participant sees the same multicast group as PX4's
  MicroXRCE-DDS agent
- No port mapping needed
- No added latency from virtual bridges or NAT
- `ROS_DOMAIN_ID` works exactly as it would on the host

# Networking Deep Dive

This section traces every byte of network traffic in the system, from Gazebo
sensor data to actuator commands, explaining the protocols, ports, and kernel
mechanisms involved.

## The Three Network Hops

During flight, a single control loop iteration involves three distinct network
hops.  With `--net host`, all three happen over the loopback interface (`lo`) at
kernel memory-copy speed --- no packets ever touch a physical NIC.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        HOST KERNEL (net namespace)                  │
│                                                                     │
│  Gazebo Server ←──── gz transport (shared memory) ────→ PX4 SITL   │
│                                                           │         │
│                                              uORB → XRCE-DDS       │
│                                              (serialized uORB msgs) │
│                                                           │         │
│                                            UDP port 8888  │         │
│                                                           ↓         │
│                                        MicroXRCE-DDS Agent          │
│                                              │                      │
│                            ┌─── DDS (RTPS) ──┘                      │
│                            │   multicast 239.255.0.1:7400           │
│                            │   + unicast UDP ephemeral ports        │
│                            ↓                                        │
│                     Controller Node (inside Docker, same namespace)  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Hop 1: Gazebo $\leftrightarrow$ PX4 (gz transport / shared memory)

PX4 SITL and the Gazebo server run as separate processes on the host.  They
communicate via **Gazebo Transport**, which uses shared memory when both
processes are on the same machine.  Messages exchanged:

| Direction | gz Topic | Content |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| Gazebo → PX4 | `/world/default/model/x500/link/base_link/sensor/imu/imu` | IMU accelerometer + gyroscope |
| Gazebo → PX4 | `/model/x500/odometry` | Ground-truth pose (for state estimator) |
| PX4 → Gazebo | `/model/x500/command/motor_speed` | Four motor velocity commands |

This hop has **zero network overhead** --- it is a memory copy between processes
mediated by the Gazebo transport library.  No sockets, no serialization beyond
protobuf encoding.

### Hop 2: PX4 $\leftrightarrow$ MicroXRCE-DDS Agent (XRCE-DDS over UDP)

PX4's internal message bus is **uORB** (micro Object Request Broker) --- a
publish-subscribe system that runs inside the PX4 process.  To expose uORB
topics to ROS 2, PX4 includes an **XRCE-DDS client** that:

1. Serializes selected uORB messages into CDR (Common Data Representation) format
2. Sends them over UDP to the **MicroXRCE-DDS Agent** on port 8888

The agent is a separate process (`MicroXRCEAgent udp4 -p 8888`) that acts as a
protocol translator:

- **Upstream** (PX4 side): speaks XRCE-DDS (a lightweight, bandwidth-efficient
  protocol designed for microcontrollers)
- **Downstream** (ROS 2 side): speaks full RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe),
  the wire protocol of DDS

The UDP connection between PX4 and the agent is on `localhost:8888`.  The
`MICROXRCE_PORT` variable in the Makefile controls this port number.

### Hop 3: MicroXRCE-DDS Agent $\leftrightarrow$ Controller Node (DDS / RTPS)

Once the agent translates a uORB message into a ROS 2 topic publication, it
enters the standard DDS communication layer.  ROS 2 Jazzy uses **Fast DDS** as
its default RMW (ROS Middleware) implementation.  The protocol works as follows:

#### Discovery (SPDP --- Simple Participant Discovery Protocol)

When a ROS 2 node starts, its DDS participant announces itself by sending a
multicast UDP packet to:

- **Address**: `239.255.0.1`
- **Port**: `7400 + (250 × ROS_DOMAIN_ID)` = `7400 + (250 × 31)` = **15150**

Every other DDS participant on the same domain listens on this multicast group.
They exchange participant information (what topics they publish/subscribe, QoS
settings, locators).

With `--net host`, the container's DDS participant sends this multicast on the
host's loopback interface, where the MicroXRCE-DDS agent (also on the host)
receives it.  Discovery completes in milliseconds.

#### Endpoint Matching (SEDP --- Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol)

After participants discover each other, they exchange endpoint information via
unicast UDP.  A "match" occurs when:

- A publisher and subscriber share the same topic name and type
- Their QoS profiles are compatible (e.g., `BEST_EFFORT` publisher matches
  `BEST_EFFORT` subscriber)

For this workspace, the QoS is:

```python
QoSProfile(
    reliability=BEST_EFFORT,     # no retransmission, lowest latency
    durability=TRANSIENT_LOCAL,  # late joiners get last sample
    history=KEEP_LAST,
    depth=1                      # only latest message buffered
)
```

**`BEST_EFFORT`** means dropped packets are not retransmitted.  For 100 Hz
real-time control, retransmitting a stale state estimate is worse than skipping
it --- the next one arrives 10 ms later with fresher data.

**`TRANSIENT_LOCAL`** means the publisher keeps its last message and delivers it
to any subscriber that joins after publication.  This is important for the
`OffboardControlMode` topic: PX4 needs to see this message immediately when the
controller starts, even if it was published a few milliseconds before PX4's
subscriber came online.

**`depth=1`** means only the most recent message is buffered.  There is no
point keeping a queue of old state estimates --- the controller always wants the
newest one.

#### Data Exchange (User Data over RTPS)

Once matched, actual topic data flows over **unicast UDP** between the agent and
the controller node.  Each message is serialized as CDR (the same binary format
used internally by DDS) and sent as a single UDP datagram.

Typical message sizes:

| Topic | Approximate Size |
|-------|-----------------|
| `VehicleOdometry` | ~200 bytes |
| `VehicleRatesSetpoint` | ~80 bytes |
| `OffboardControlMode` | ~40 bytes |
| `TrajectorySetpoint` | ~120 bytes |

At 100 Hz, the total bandwidth is roughly:

$$
(200 + 80 + 40 + 120) \times 100 \approx 44\text{ KB/s}
$$

This is negligible --- well within loopback throughput (typically 10+ GB/s).

## `ROS_DOMAIN_ID` --- DDS Traffic Isolation

```makefile
-e ROS_DOMAIN_ID=31
```

DDS domain IDs partition the multicast discovery space.  Participants on
domain 31 use multicast port 15150 (as computed above); participants on domain 0
use port 7400.  They never see each other's discovery announcements.

This is important if you have other ROS 2 systems running on the same machine
(e.g., a second PX4 SITL instance, or a ROS 2 tutorial).  Domain 31 prevents
cross-talk.

The same `ROS_DOMAIN_ID` must be set for:

1. The MicroXRCE-DDS Agent (inherits from the host shell environment)
2. The controller container (`-e ROS_DOMAIN_ID=31`)

PX4 itself does not use `ROS_DOMAIN_ID` --- it speaks XRCE-DDS to the agent,
not full DDS.  The agent handles the domain ID mapping.

## What Would Break Without `--net host`

If you used Docker's default bridge network instead:

| Problem | Why |
|---------|-----|
| **Multicast blocked** | Docker's bridge (`docker0`) does not forward multicast by default.  DDS discovery fails --- nodes cannot find each other. |
| **Port mapping required** | You would need `-p 7400:7400/udp -p 15150:15150/udp` plus ranges of ephemeral ports for data exchange.  DDS uses dynamic port allocation, making this impractical. |
| **NAT latency** | Each packet traverses Docker's `iptables` NAT rules.  For 100 Hz control, even 0.1 ms added latency matters. |
| **Localhost mismatch** | The agent listens on `localhost:8888` from PX4's perspective.  With bridge networking, the container's `localhost` is different from the host's `localhost`. |

The only alternatives to `--net host` for DDS are:

- **Cyclone DDS with unicast-only config** (disable multicast, manually list peer
  IPs) --- complex, fragile
- **macvlan/ipvlan** network --- gives the container its own IP on the host LAN,
  but adds unnecessary complexity for a single-machine setup
- **Fast DDS Discovery Server** --- replaces multicast with a centralized
  discovery server, but adds another process to manage

`--net host` is the simplest and most performant choice for single-machine
SITL simulation.

## Latency Budget

For a sense of scale, here is the approximate latency of one complete control
cycle (state estimate → control command → actuator):

| Segment | Latency |
|---------|---------|
| Gazebo → PX4 (shared memory) | < 0.01 ms |
| PX4 state estimator processing | ~0.5 ms |
| PX4 → Agent (XRCE-DDS, localhost UDP) | < 0.05 ms |
| Agent → Controller (DDS, localhost UDP) | < 0.05 ms |
| Controller computation (NR: 0.4 ms, NMPC: 2 ms) | 0.4--2.0 ms |
| Controller → Agent → PX4 (return path) | < 0.1 ms |
| PX4 rate controller + motor mixing | ~0.2 ms |
| **Total round-trip** | **~1.3--3.0 ms** |

At 100 Hz (10 ms period), even the NMPC controller has ~7 ms of headroom before
the next cycle.  Networking overhead (`< 0.2 ms` total) is negligible compared
to computation.


### The bind mount

```
-v $(WS_ROOT):/workspace
```

Your entire repo directory appears at `/workspace` inside the container.  This
is a **bind mount**, not a copy --- changes on either side are instantly visible
to the other.  This means:

- You edit source files on the host with your normal editor/IDE
- `colcon build` inside the container compiles them
- Build artifacts (`build/`, `install/`, `log/`) appear on the host filesystem
- CSV logs written by controllers inside the container are immediately accessible
  on the host for analysis


# How Commands Execute Inside the Container

Every Makefile target that runs inside the container uses the same pattern:

```makefile
docker exec $(CONTAINER_NAME) bash -lc "..."
```

Or for interactive targets:

```makefile
docker exec -it $(CONTAINER_NAME) bash -lc "..."
```

### Why `bash -lc`?

- **`-l` (login shell)**: Triggers sourcing of `/etc/profile.d/ros_setup.sh`,
  which sets up the full ROS 2 + px4_msgs + acados environment.
- **`-c "..."`**: Executes the given command string, then exits.

Without `-l`, none of the ROS 2 environment variables would be set, and
`ros2 run` would fail with "package not found."

### The `colcon build` pattern (C++ targets)

C++ controller targets (e.g., `run_newton_raphson_cpp`) include an inline build
step:

```makefile
docker exec -it $(CONTAINER_NAME) bash -lc \
    "cd /workspace && colcon build \
       --symlink-install \
       --packages-up-to newton_raphson_px4_cpp \
       --cmake-args -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
                    -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON && \
     source /workspace/install/setup.bash && \
     ros2 run newton_raphson_px4_cpp run_node ..."
```

This ensures:

1. The C++ package is compiled with latest source changes
2. The install overlay is re-sourced to pick up newly built packages
3. The node runs with the freshly compiled binary

Python targets skip the build step because `--symlink-install` creates symlinks
from `install/` back to `src/`, so Python source edits take effect immediately.


# The Full System Architecture

Putting it all together, here is what happens when you run
`make fly CONTROLLER=newton_raphson TRAJECTORY=fig8_horz HEADLESS=1`:

```
  HOST                                   CONTAINER
  ────                                   ─────────
  1. fly_pipeline.py starts

  2. PX4 SITL launches (HEADLESS=1)
     ├── Gazebo server (physics only)
     └── PX4 firmware (lockstep)

  3. MicroXRCE-DDS Agent starts
     (UDP port 8888)

  4. Docker container starts              ← make run
     (--net host, bind mount)

  5.                                      colcon build (if C++)
                                          source install/setup.bash

  6.                                      ros2 run <controller> run_node
                                            │
                                            ├── Subscribes to:
                                            │   /fmu/out/vehicle_odometry
                                            │   /fmu/out/vehicle_status_v1
                                            │   /fmu/out/rc_channels
                                            │
                                            ├── Publishes to:
                                            │   /fmu/in/offboard_control_mode
                                            │   /fmu/in/trajectory_setpoint
                                            │   /fmu/in/vehicle_rates_setpoint
                                            │   /fmu/in/vehicle_command
                                            │
                                            └── Flight phases:
                                                HOVER → CUSTOM → RETURN → LAND

  7. Controller exits after landing

  8. fly_pipeline.py collects logs
     and computes RMSE analysis

  9. SITL + bridge + container cleaned up
```

### Data flow during the CUSTOM phase (100 Hz loop)

```
Gazebo physics  →  PX4 state estimator  →  uORB VehicleOdometry
    │                                           │
    │                                     MicroXRCE-DDS Agent
    │                                           │
    │                                     DDS (localhost UDP)
    │                                           │
    │                                     Controller Node
    │                                       ├── Read state (pos, vel, quat)
    │                                       ├── Evaluate reference trajectory
    │                                       ├── Compute control (NR/NMPC/etc.)
    │                                       └── Publish thrust + body rates
    │                                           │
    │                                     DDS (localhost UDP)
    │                                           │
    │                                     MicroXRCE-DDS Agent
    │                                           │
    │                                     uORB VehicleRatesSetpoint
    │                                           │
    └───────────  PX4 rate controller  ←────────┘
                       │
                  Motor mixing → Gazebo actuator commands → physics step
```


# Image Size Breakdown

| Layer | Approximate Size |
|-------|-----------------|
| `osrf/ros:jazzy-ros-base` | ~700 MB |
| System apt packages | ~200 MB |
| px4_msgs (install only) | ~15 MB |
| acados (libs + headers + templates) | ~150 MB |
| JAX + scipy + numpy + friends | ~600 MB |
| immrax + linrax | ~50 MB |
| **Total** | **~1.7 GB** |

The previous `desktop-full` base added ~1.8 GB of unused GUI packages.


# Key Design Decisions Summarized

| Decision | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| Container runs controllers only, not PX4/Gazebo | Avoids GPU passthrough, keeps image small |
| `--net host` instead of bridge networking | DDS multicast works transparently, zero latency overhead |
| `ros-base` instead of `desktop-full` | 1.8 GB savings; no GUI tools needed |
| px4_msgs baked into image | Avoids rebuild on every container restart |
| acados built from source | PyPI wheel does not include qpOASES backend |
| Bind mount for workspace | Edit on host, build in container; logs instantly accessible |
| `bash -lc` for all exec commands | Guarantees ROS 2 overlay chain is sourced |
| `--symlink-install` for colcon | Python edits take effect without rebuilding |
| `CMD ["bash"]` with `-itd` | Container stays alive as a long-running shell, ready for exec |
